3	description: Whole-project read-only audit of all .lean code, with no strategy bias. Per-file checklist of outdated comments, suspect definitions, dead-end proofs, bad Lean practices. Critical pushback on excuse-comments ("temporary wrong def", "will fix later") — treats them as red flags, not workflow.

14	      - no `.lean` file under the project tree was modified this iter
15	        (the prover phase committed no edits — check via

17	      - my prior verdict had no must-fix-this-iter findings.

34	---
35	
36	# Lean Auditor
37	
38	You read **every `.lean` file in the project** and produce a per-file checklist of outdated comments, suspect definitions, dead-end proofs, and bad Lean practices. You are **read-only** and your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
39	
40	You are **highly recommended in the review phase**: the review agent dispatches you whenever any `.lean` file has been modified this iter, OR whenever the prior verdict had live must-fix findings. Your output flags issues the prover and plan agents may have missed.
41	
42	## Stance: no strategic bias
43	
44	You audit the Lean **as Lean**. Your directive will NOT include the project strategy, the blueprint, or "what we are trying to prove" — and that is intentional. The point is to detect mathematically-wrong definitions and dead-end proofs *without* being swayed by what the project assumes should be true. A definition is wrong if it is wrong by reading the Lean alone (e.g. its body contradicts standard mathematical usage of the named concept); you do not need to know the strategy to spot that.
45	
46	This stance is also what makes you **critical of excuse-comments**. Lean-side comments like:
47	
48	- `-- We decided to use a temporary wrong definition; will fix it later.`
49	- `-- This proof is incomplete but works for now.`
50	- `-- TODO: replace before merging.`
51	- `-- Stand-in definition until we figure out the right one.`
52	
53	are RED FLAGS, not project workflow. Authors writing such comments are admitting the code is wrong; the comments cannot be allowed to silence the alarm. Flag every such comment with severity **major** at minimum — and **critical** when the named declaration is load-bearing.
54	
55	## Directive Format
56	
57	```markdown
58	# Lean Auditor Directive
59	
60	## Slug
61	<slug>
62	
63	## Scope (files)
64	<"all" by default. Optionally a list of file paths if the review agent wants to narrow scope; never expand beyond the project.>
65	
66	## Focus areas (optional)
67	<directories or namespaces to pay extra attention to. Bias toward thoroughness — do not skip the rest of the project just because focus is named.>
68	
69	## Known issues
70	<things the review agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
71	```
72	
73	The directive **does not** include STRATEGY.md, PROGRESS.md, references, or a description of what the project is trying to prove. That is by design.
74	
75	## What you do
76	
77	1. Read your directive.
78	2. **List every `.lean` file** under the project. Use `find` or `ls -R` — do not rely on import graphs (some files may be temporarily orphaned).
79	3. **For each file** (every one, no scope shortcuts):
80	   - Read the whole file.
81	   - For each declaration: is the signature reasonable as Lean? Is the body suspect (uses `axiom`, `Classical.choice _`, `sorry` with a body that looks like fake content, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial claim)?
82	   - For each comment: is it stale (referencing removed code or old strategies)? Is it an *excuse-comment* (see above)? Is it accurate about the code below it?
83	   - For each proof: is the tactic chain reasonable for the goal type, or does it look like it is going in the wrong direction (e.g. unfolding to manipulate syntax instead of using the available structure lemmas)?
84	   - Note any bad Lean practices: misuse of universe polymorphism, redundant typeclasses, deprecated tactics, anti-patterns like proving `False`-style lemmas to bypass infrastructure.
85	4. **Produce a per-file checklist** + a flagged-issues block grouped by severity.
86	
87	You may use:
88	- `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools (read-only): hover, signature lookup, diagnostics. Useful to verify a declaration's actual type.
89	- `sorry_analyzer` if available, to get sorry counts per file.
90	- Standard `Read` / `Grep` for code reading.
91	
92	You may NOT modify any project file. Even when a fix is obvious.
93	
94	## Report format
95	
96	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/lean-auditor-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
97	
98	```markdown
99	# Lean Audit Report
100	
101	## Slug
102	<slug>
103	
104	## Iteration
105	<NNN>
106	
107	## Scope
108	- files audited: <N>
109	- files skipped (per directive): <N> — <reason>
110	
111	## Per-file checklist
112	
113	### <path/to/file.lean>
114	- **outdated comments**: <N flagged> (or "none")
115	- **suspect definitions**: <N flagged> (or "none")
116	- **dead-end proofs**: <N flagged> (or "none")
117	- **bad practices**: <N flagged> (or "none")
118	- **excuse-comments**: <N flagged> (or "none")
119	- **notes**:
120	  - <one bullet per finding, naming line number where relevant>
121	  - ...
122	
123	### <next file>
124	- ...
125	
126	(One block per file. Cover every file, including the ones with no findings — they get `none` everywhere and `notes: -`.)
127	
128	## Must-fix-this-iter
129	
130	Apply verbatim. Every one of the following lands here automatically, no exceptions, no under-classification:
131	
132	- **Excuse-comments** on any declaration: `-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- placeholder`, `-- temporary`, `-- wrong but works`, `-- will fix later`. These are admissions that the code is wrong; treat them as wrong.
133	- **Weakened-wrong definitions**: a `def Foo := <something structurally different>` standing in for the real concept (e.g. `Scheme.LineBundle := CommRing.Pic …` when the real definition is an invertible quasi-coherent sheaf).
134	- **Parallel APIs of existing Mathlib**: a project file that copy-and-modifies a Mathlib definition (changing one type-class or argument) instead of instancing the existing Mathlib version. Bridge lemmas needed to consume it are the symptom.
135	- **Suspect bodies on substantive claims**: `:= sorry` on a load-bearing claim, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial statement, `:= Classical.choice _` without authorization.
136	- **Axioms** on non-trivial claims that the project hasn't explicitly authorized in the strategy.
137	
138	A finding that meets any of the above lands at must-fix-this-iter — even if classifying it as "major" would feel more diplomatic. Soft severity is how wrong code hardens into the project.
139	
140	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>. Why must-fix: <one line>.
141	- ...
142	
143	## Major
144	
145	Findings that are real issues but don't meet the must-fix bar — typically stale comments that don't actively mislead, naming drift, minor code smells.
146	
147	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>.
148	- ...
149	
150	## Minor
151	
152	Low-impact observations.
153	
154	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>.
155	- ...
156	
157	## Excuse-comments (always called out separately)
158	
159	If any excuse-comments were flagged, list each one verbatim with its file:line. These deserve special visibility because they document the project lying to itself.
160	
161	- `Foo/Bar.lean:42`: "TODO: replace with the real definition" (attached to `Foo.bar`, which is a load-bearing definition in `Baz.lean`). Severity: critical.
162	- ...
163	
164	## Severity summary
165	
166	- **must-fix-this-iter**: <N> — these block downstream work in their files until addressed (see plan.md's per-file gate).
167	- **major**: <N>
168	- **minor**: <N>
169	- **excuse-comments**: <N> (also counted under must-fix-this-iter above; called out separately because they document the project lying to itself).
170	
171	Overall verdict: one sentence.
172	```
173	
174	**Strict severity reminder.** The plan agent's gate treats must-fix-this-iter as blocking. If you under-classify a wrong-decision finding to keep the loop's momentum, you are working against the project — wrong code accumulates faster than reviewers can catch it later. When in doubt, classify higher, not lower.
175	
176	## Return value
177	
178	Your final assistant message:
179	
180	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> files audited, <M> issues (critical/major/minor: a/b/c)`
181	- The path to your full report.
182	
183	## Reminders
184	
185	- **Read every file. No scope shortcuts.**
186	- **No strategic context.** Your audit is unbiased by design.
187	- **You are read-only.** No project source, no blueprint, no state files (except your own report).
188	- **Excuse-comments are red flags.** Critical or major severity, every time.
189	- **Per-file checklist is the primary output.** Cover every file; the format is fixed.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.

87	mono / epi respectively. This is the `lean_aux` infrastructure behind
88	`InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact_resolvesMiddle`. -/
89	lemma quasiIso_τ₂ (h₁ : QuasiIso φ.τ₁) (h₃ : QuasiIso φ.τ₃)
90	    (hbMono : ∀ i, (∀ k, ¬ c.Rel k i) → Mono (φ.τ₂.f i))
91	    (hbEpi : ∀ i, (∀ j, ¬ c.Rel i j) → Epi (φ.τ₂.f i)) :
92	    QuasiIso φ.τ₂ := by
93	  have hI1 : ∀ d, IsIso (homologyMap φ.τ₁ d) := fun d => by
94	    rw [← quasiIsoAt_iff_isIso_homologyMap]; exact (quasiIso_iff φ.τ₁).1 h₁ d
95	  have hI3 : ∀ d, IsIso (homologyMap φ.τ₃ d) := fun d => by
96	    rw [← quasiIsoAt_iff_isIso_homologyMap]; exact (quasiIso_iff φ.τ₃).1 h₃ d
97	  have hE1 : ∀ d, Epi (homologyMap φ.τ₁ d) := fun d => have := hI1 d; inferInstance
98	  have hM1 : ∀ d, Mono (homologyMap φ.τ₁ d) := fun d => have := hI1 d; inferInstance
99	  have hE3 : ∀ d, Epi (homologyMap φ.τ₃ d) := fun d => have := hI3 d; inferInstance
100	  have hM3 : ∀ d, Mono (homologyMap φ.τ₃ d) := fun d => have := hI3 d; inferInstance
101	  rw [quasiIso_iff]
102	  intro i
103	  rw [quasiIsoAt_iff_isIso_homologyMap]
104	  have hEpi : Epi (homologyMap φ.τ₂ i) := by
105	    by_cases hi : ∃ j, c.Rel i j
106	    · obtain ⟨j, hij⟩ := hi
107	      apply epi_of_epi_of_epi_of_mono
108	        ((δlastFunctor ⋙ δlastFunctor).map (mapComposableArrows₅ φ hS₁ hS₂ i j hij))
109	      · exact (composableArrows₅_exact hS₁ i j hij).δlast.δlast
110	      · exact (composableArrows₅_exact hS₂ i j hij).δlast.δlast
111	      · exact hE1 i
112	      · exact hE3 i
113	      · exact hM1 j
114	    · have hi' : ∀ j, ¬ c.Rel i j := fun j hj => hi ⟨j, hj⟩
115	      have := hbEpi i hi'
116	      exact epi_homologyMap_of_epi_of_not_rel φ.τ₂ i hi'
117	  have hMono : Mono (homologyMap φ.τ₂ i) := by
118	    by_cases hi : ∃ k, c.Rel k i
119	    · obtain ⟨k, hki⟩ := hi
120	      apply mono_of_epi_of_mono_of_mono
121	        ((δ₀Functor ⋙ δ₀Functor).map (mapComposableArrows₅ φ hS₁ hS₂ k i hki))
122	      · exact (composableArrows₅_exact hS₁ k i hki).δ₀.δ₀
123	      · exact (composableArrows₅_exact hS₂ k i hki).δ₀.δ₀
124	      · exact hE3 k
125	      · exact hM1 i
126	      · exact hM3 i
127	    · have hi' : ∀ k, ¬ c.Rel k i := fun k hk => hi ⟨k, hk⟩
128	      have := hbMono i hi'
129	      exact mono_homologyMap_of_mono_of_not_rel φ.τ₂ i hi'
130	  exact isIso_of_mono_of_epi _

145	functor of `G` vanishes at `J`:
146	```
147	(Rᵏ⁺¹ G)(J) = 0   for all k : ℕ.

149	The index-shifted quantifier `k + 1` matches the statement of
150	`Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ` and avoids an inequality

153	Blueprint: `CategoryTheory.Functor.IsRightAcyclic` (`def:right_acyclic`).
154	-/
155	class Functor.IsRightAcyclic (G : 𝒜 ⥤ ℬ) [G.Additive] (J : 𝒜) : Prop where
156	  vanish : ∀ k : ℕ, Limits.IsZero ((G.rightDerived (k + 1)).obj J)

161	    (G : 𝒜 ⥤ ℬ) [G.Additive] (J : 𝒜) [Injective J] : Functor.IsRightAcyclic G J where
162	  vanish k := Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ G k J

252	lemma mono_biprod_lift_factorThru_of_exact {S : ShortComplex 𝒜} (hS : S.Exact) [Mono S.f]
253	    {P Q : 𝒜} [Injective P] (α : S.X₁ ⟶ P) [Mono α] (γ : S.X₃ ⟶ Q) [Mono γ] :
254	    Mono (biprod.lift (Injective.factorThru α S.f) (S.g ≫ γ)) := by
255	  rw [Preadditive.mono_iff_cancel_zero]
256	  intro T x hx
257	  have h1 : x ≫ Injective.factorThru α S.f = 0 := by simpa using hx =≫ biprod.fst
258	  have h2 : x ≫ S.g ≫ γ = 0 := by simpa using hx =≫ biprod.snd
259	  have hxg : x ≫ S.g = 0 := by rw [← cancel_mono γ, zero_comp, Category.assoc]; exact h2
260	  have hyf : hS.lift x hxg ≫ S.f = x := hS.lift_f x hxg
261	  have hya : hS.lift x hxg ≫ α = 0 := by
262	    have h3 : hS.lift x hxg ≫ S.f ≫ Injective.factorThru α S.f = 0 := by
263	      rw [← Category.assoc, hyf]; exact h1
264	    rwa [Injective.comp_factorThru] at h3
265	  have hy0 : hS.lift x hxg = 0 := by rw [← cancel_mono α, zero_comp]; exact hya
266	  rw [← hyf, hy0, zero_comp]

269	
270	This block builds the *structural* core of the dual Horseshoe Lemma as a general, injective-free
271	construction. Given two cochain complexes `K`, `L` and a degreewise family
272	`τ n : L.X n ⟶ K.X (n+1)` satisfying the cocycle identity
273	`L.d n (n+1) ≫ τ (n+1) = -(τ n ≫ K.d (n+1) (n+2))`, the biproduct objects `K.X n ⊞ L.X n` with the
274	twisted matrix differential `[[d_K, τ], [0, d_L]]` form a cochain complex `twistedBiprod τ hτ`,
275	the coprojection `K ⟶ twistedBiprod` and projection `twistedBiprod ⟶ L` are chain maps, and every
276	degree is the canonical split short exact sequence of the biproduct.
277	
278	This is exactly the content of the blueprint sub-lemmas `lem:horseshoe_dComp` (the differential
279	squares to zero) and `lem:horseshoe_chainMap` (the coprojection/projection are chain maps and the
280	degrees split); it is isolated here free of injectivity because the only inputs are the cocycle
281	identity and the biproduct structure. The horseshoe specialises it to `K = I_A.cocomplex`,
282	`L = I_C.cocomplex` once the twist `τ` has been produced by injectivity. -/

284	section TwistedBiprod

286	omit [HasInjectiveResolutions 𝒜]

288	variable {K L : CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ} (τ : ∀ n, L.X n ⟶ K.X (n + 1))

290	/-- The twisted matrix differential `[[d_K, τ], [0, d_L]]` on the degreewise biproduct
291	`K.X n ⊞ L.X n ⟶ K.X (n+1) ⊞ L.X (n+1)`. -/
292	noncomputable def twistedBiprodD (n : ℕ) :
293	    (K.X n ⊞ L.X n) ⟶ (K.X (n + 1) ⊞ L.X (n + 1)) :=
294	  biprod.lift (biprod.fst ≫ K.d n (n + 1) + biprod.snd ≫ τ n) (biprod.snd ≫ L.d n (n + 1))
295	
296	@[reassoc (attr := simp)]
297	lemma twistedBiprodD_fst (n : ℕ) :
298	    twistedBiprodD τ n ≫ biprod.fst = biprod.fst ≫ K.d n (n + 1) + biprod.snd ≫ τ n := by
299	  simp [twistedBiprodD]
300	
301	@[reassoc (attr := simp)]
302	lemma twistedBiprodD_snd (n : ℕ) :
303	    twistedBiprodD τ n ≫ biprod.snd = biprod.snd ≫ L.d n (n + 1) := by
304	  simp [twistedBiprodD]
305	
306	variable (hτ : ∀ n, L.d n (n + 1) ≫ τ (n + 1) = -(τ n ≫ K.d (n + 1) (n + 2)))
307	
308	include hτ in
309	lemma twistedBiprodD_comp (n : ℕ) :
310	    twistedBiprodD τ n ≫ twistedBiprodD τ (n + 1) = 0 := by
311	  apply biprod.hom_ext
312	  · simp only [Category.assoc, twistedBiprodD_fst, Preadditive.comp_add,
313	      twistedBiprodD_fst_assoc, twistedBiprodD_snd_assoc, zero_comp]
314	    rw [Preadditive.add_comp, Category.assoc, Category.assoc, HomologicalComplex.d_comp_d,
315	      comp_zero, zero_add, ← Preadditive.comp_add, hτ n, add_neg_cancel, comp_zero]
316	  · simp only [Category.assoc, twistedBiprodD_snd, twistedBiprodD_snd_assoc, zero_comp]
317	    rw [HomologicalComplex.d_comp_d, comp_zero]
318	
319	/-- The twisted biproduct cochain complex with differential `[[d_K, τ], [0, d_L]]`. -/
320	noncomputable def twistedBiprod : CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ :=
321	  CochainComplex.of (fun n => K.X n ⊞ L.X n) (twistedBiprodD τ) (twistedBiprodD_comp τ hτ)
322	
323	@[simp]
324	lemma twistedBiprod_X (n : ℕ) : (twistedBiprod τ hτ).X n = (K.X n ⊞ L.X n) := rfl
325	
326	@[simp]
327	lemma twistedBiprod_d (n : ℕ) : (twistedBiprod τ hτ).d n (n + 1) = twistedBiprodD τ n :=
328	  CochainComplex.of_d _ _ _ _
329	
330	/-- The coprojection `K ⟶ twistedBiprod τ hτ`, degreewise `biprod.inl`. -/
331	noncomputable def twistedBiprodInl : K ⟶ twistedBiprod τ hτ where
332	  f n := biprod.inl
333	  comm' i j hij := by
334	    obtain rfl : i + 1 = j := hij
335	    simp only [twistedBiprod_d]
336	    apply biprod.hom_ext <;> simp
337	
338	/-- The projection `twistedBiprod τ hτ ⟶ L`, degreewise `biprod.snd`. -/
339	noncomputable def twistedBiprodSnd : twistedBiprod τ hτ ⟶ L where
340	  f n := biprod.snd
341	  comm' i j hij := by
342	    obtain rfl : i + 1 = j := hij
343	    simp [twistedBiprod_d]
344	
345	@[simp]
346	lemma twistedBiprodInl_f (n : ℕ) : (twistedBiprodInl τ hτ).f n = biprod.inl := rfl
347	
348	@[simp]
349	lemma twistedBiprodSnd_f (n : ℕ) : (twistedBiprodSnd τ hτ).f n = biprod.snd := rfl
350	
351	lemma twistedBiprodInl_comp_Snd : twistedBiprodInl τ hτ ≫ twistedBiprodSnd τ hτ = 0 := by
352	  ext n
353	  simp
354	
355	/-- Each degree of `0 → K → twistedBiprod → L → 0` is the canonical split short exact sequence of
356	the biproduct `K.X n ⊞ L.X n`. -/
357	noncomputable def twistedBiprodSplitting (n : ℕ) :
358	    ((ShortComplex.mk _ _ (twistedBiprodInl_comp_Snd τ hτ)).map
359	      (HomologicalComplex.eval 𝒜 (ComplexShape.up ℕ) n)).Splitting where
360	  r := biprod.fst
361	  s := biprod.inr
362	  id := by simpa using biprod.total
363	
364	end TwistedBiprod

366	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — the horseshoe twist family
367	
368	Given a short exact sequence `ses : 0 → A → B → C → 0` and chosen injective resolutions
369	`I_A`, `I_C`, this block constructs the off-diagonal twist family
370	`τ n : I_C.X n ⟶ I_A.X (n+1)` together with the augmentation first component, satisfying the
371	cocycle identity `d_C n ≫ τ (n+1) = -(τ n ≫ d_A (n+1))`. Each `τ` is produced by the universal
372	lifting property of injectives (`Injective.factorThru` / `ShortComplex.Exact.descToInjective`)
373	against the cosyzygy monomorphisms of `I_C`. This is the blueprint sub-lemma `lem:horseshoe_twist`
374	(the recursion kernel). Combined with the `twistedBiprod` construction above it yields the middle
375	complex of the dual Horseshoe Lemma. -/
376	
377	namespace InjectiveResolution
378	
379	section OfShortExact
380	
381	variable {ses : ShortComplex 𝒜} (hses : ses.ShortExact)
382	  (I_A : InjectiveResolution ses.X₁) (I_C : InjectiveResolution ses.X₃)
383	
384	/-- First component `B ⟶ I_A^0` of the horseshoe augmentation: the injective extension of the
385	augmentation `A ⟶ I_A^0` along the monomorphism `A ↪ B`. -/
386	noncomputable def horseshoeβ₁ : ses.X₂ ⟶ I_A.cocomplex.X 0 :=
387	  @Injective.factorThru _ _ _ _ _ (I_A.injective 0) (I_A.ι.f 0) ses.f hses.mono_f
388	
389	@[reassoc (attr := simp)]
390	lemma f_comp_horseshoeβ₁ : ses.f ≫ horseshoeβ₁ hses I_A = I_A.ι.f 0 :=
391	  @Injective.comp_factorThru _ _ _ _ _ (I_A.injective 0) (I_A.ι.f 0) ses.f hses.mono_f
392	
393	/-- Auxiliary map `C ⟶ I_A^1` through which `β₁ ≫ d_A^0` factors (since it kills `A`). -/
394	noncomputable def horseshoeH : ses.X₃ ⟶ I_A.cocomplex.X 1 :=
395	  hses.exact.descToInjective (horseshoeβ₁ hses I_A ≫ I_A.cocomplex.d 0 1) (by
396	    rw [← Category.assoc, f_comp_horseshoeβ₁]; exact I_A.ι_f_zero_comp_complex_d)
397	
398	@[reassoc (attr := simp)]
399	lemma g_comp_horseshoeH :
400	    ses.g ≫ horseshoeH hses I_A = horseshoeβ₁ hses I_A ≫ I_A.cocomplex.d 0 1 :=
401	  hses.exact.comp_descToInjective _ _
402	
403	lemma horseshoeH_comp_d : horseshoeH hses I_A ≫ I_A.cocomplex.d 1 2 = 0 := by
404	  haveI := hses.epi_g
405	  rw [← cancel_epi ses.g, comp_zero, ← Category.assoc, g_comp_horseshoeH, Category.assoc,
406	    HomologicalComplex.d_comp_d, comp_zero]
407	
408	/-- The base twist `τ⁰ : I_C^0 ⟶ I_A^1`, extending `-(C ⟶ I_A^1)` along `C ↪ I_C^0`. -/
409	noncomputable def horseshoeτZero : I_C.cocomplex.X 0 ⟶ I_A.cocomplex.X 1 :=
410	  @Injective.factorThru _ _ _ _ _ (I_A.injective 1) (-horseshoeH hses I_A) (I_C.ι.f 0)
411	    (mono_of_isLimit_fork I_C.isLimitKernelFork)
412	
413	@[reassoc (attr := simp)]
414	lemma ιC_comp_horseshoeτZero :
415	    I_C.ι.f 0 ≫ horseshoeτZero hses I_A I_C = -horseshoeH hses I_A :=
416	  @Injective.comp_factorThru _ _ _ _ _ (I_A.injective 1) (-horseshoeH hses I_A) (I_C.ι.f 0)
417	    (mono_of_isLimit_fork I_C.isLimitKernelFork)
418	
419	lemma horseshoeτZero_hf :
420	    I_C.ι.f 0 ≫ (-(horseshoeτZero hses I_A I_C ≫ I_A.cocomplex.d 1 2)) = 0 := by
421	  have e : (-horseshoeH hses I_A) ≫ I_A.cocomplex.d 1 2 = 0 := by
422	    rw [Preadditive.neg_comp, horseshoeH_comp_d, neg_zero]
423	  rw [Preadditive.comp_neg, neg_eq_zero, ← Category.assoc, ιC_comp_horseshoeτZero]
424	  exact e
425	
426	/-- The recursion carrying, at each degree `n`, consecutive twists `τⁿ`, `τⁿ⁺¹` together with the
427	cocycle identity `d_C^n ≫ τⁿ⁺¹ = -(τⁿ ≫ d_A^{n+1})`. The step uses the lifting property of the
428	injective `I_A^{n+3}` against the cosyzygy mono of `I_C` (`exact_succ`); the base uses the
429	augmentation exactness `exact₀`. -/
430	noncomputable def twistPair : (n : ℕ) →
431	    Σ' (t0 : I_C.cocomplex.X n ⟶ I_A.cocomplex.X (n + 1))
432	       (_t1 : I_C.cocomplex.X (n + 1) ⟶ I_A.cocomplex.X (n + 2)),
433	       I_C.cocomplex.d n (n + 1) ≫ _t1 = -(t0 ≫ I_A.cocomplex.d (n + 1) (n + 2))
434	  | 0 => ⟨horseshoeτZero hses I_A I_C,
435	      I_C.exact₀.descToInjective (-(horseshoeτZero hses I_A I_C ≫ I_A.cocomplex.d 1 2))
436	        (horseshoeτZero_hf hses I_A I_C),
437	      I_C.exact₀.comp_descToInjective _ _⟩
438	  | (n + 1) =>
439	      let p := twistPair n
440	      ⟨p.2.1,
441	        (I_C.exact_succ n).descToInjective (-(p.2.1 ≫ I_A.cocomplex.d (n + 2) (n + 3))) (by
442	          have e : (-(p.1 ≫ I_A.cocomplex.d (n + 1) (n + 2))) ≫ I_A.cocomplex.d (n + 2) (n + 3)
443	              = 0 := by
444	            rw [Preadditive.neg_comp, Category.assoc, HomologicalComplex.d_comp_d, comp_zero,
445	              neg_zero]
446	          rw [Preadditive.comp_neg, neg_eq_zero, ← Category.assoc, p.2.2]
447	          exact e),
448	        (I_C.exact_succ n).comp_descToInjective _ _⟩
449	
450	/-- The horseshoe off-diagonal twist family `τⁿ : I_C^n ⟶ I_A^{n+1}`. -/
451	noncomputable def horseshoeτ (n : ℕ) : I_C.cocomplex.X n ⟶ I_A.cocomplex.X (n + 1) :=
452	  (twistPair hses I_A I_C n).1
453	
454	/-- The cocycle identity for the horseshoe twist: `d_C^n ≫ τⁿ⁺¹ = -(τⁿ ≫ d_A^{n+1})`. -/
455	lemma horseshoeτ_cocycle (n : ℕ) :
456	    I_C.cocomplex.d n (n + 1) ≫ horseshoeτ hses I_A I_C (n + 1) =
457	      -(horseshoeτ hses I_A I_C n ≫ I_A.cocomplex.d (n + 1) (n + 2)) :=
458	  (twistPair hses I_A I_C n).2.2
459	
460	/-- The middle cochain complex `I_B` of the horseshoe: the twisted biproduct of `I_A` and `I_C`
461	along the horseshoe twist family. -/
462	noncomputable def horseshoeMid : CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ :=
463	  twistedBiprod (horseshoeτ hses I_A I_C) (horseshoeτ_cocycle hses I_A I_C)
464	
465	/-- The short complex `0 → I_A → I_B → I_C → 0` of the horseshoe, with the coprojection and
466	projection chain maps. -/
467	noncomputable def horseshoeSES : ShortComplex (CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ) :=
468	  ShortComplex.mk _ _
469	    (twistedBiprodInl_comp_Snd (horseshoeτ hses I_A I_C) (horseshoeτ_cocycle hses I_A I_C))
470	
471	/-- Each degree of the horseshoe short complex is the canonical biproduct splitting. -/
472	noncomputable def horseshoeSES_splitting (n : ℕ) :
473	    ((horseshoeSES hses I_A I_C).map
474	      (HomologicalComplex.eval 𝒜 (ComplexShape.up ℕ) n)).Splitting :=
475	  twistedBiprodSplitting (horseshoeτ hses I_A I_C) (horseshoeτ_cocycle hses I_A I_C) n
476	
477	/-- The horseshoe short complex `0 → I_A → I_B → I_C → 0` is short exact (degreewise split). -/
478	lemma horseshoeSES_shortExact : (horseshoeSES hses I_A I_C).ShortExact :=
479	  shortExact_of_degreewise_splitting (horseshoeSES_splitting hses I_A I_C)
480	
481	@[simp]
482	lemma horseshoeτ_zero : horseshoeτ hses I_A I_C 0 = horseshoeτZero hses I_A I_C := rfl
483	
484	/-- Clean-domain degree-0 augmentation map of `I_C` (definitionally `I_C.ι.f 0`, but with syntactic
485	domain `ses.X₃` so it composes cleanly on the left with `ses.g`; the bundled `I_C.ι.f 0` carries the
486	single-complex domain `((single₀).obj ses.X₃).X 0`, which blocks rewriting under `ses.g ≫ -`). -/
487	noncomputable def ιC0 : ses.X₃ ⟶ I_C.cocomplex.X 0 := I_C.ι.f 0
488	
489	lemma ιC0_comp_d : ιC0 I_C ≫ I_C.cocomplex.d 0 1 = 0 := I_C.ι_f_zero_comp_complex_d
490	
491	lemma ιC0_comp_τZero :
492	    ιC0 I_C ≫ horseshoeτZero hses I_A I_C = -horseshoeH hses I_A :=
493	  ιC_comp_horseshoeτZero hses I_A I_C
494	
495	/-- The horseshoe augmentation `β : B ⟶ I_A^0 ⊞ I_C^0`. -/
496	noncomputable def horseshoeβ : ses.X₂ ⟶ I_A.cocomplex.X 0 ⊞ I_C.cocomplex.X 0 :=
497	  biprod.lift (horseshoeβ₁ hses I_A) (ses.g ≫ ιC0 I_C)
498	
499	@[reassoc (attr := simp)]
500	lemma horseshoeβ_fst : horseshoeβ hses I_A I_C ≫ biprod.fst = horseshoeβ₁ hses I_A := by
501	  rw [horseshoeβ, biprod.lift_fst]
502	
503	@[reassoc (attr := simp)]
504	lemma horseshoeβ_snd : horseshoeβ hses I_A I_C ≫ biprod.snd = ses.g ≫ ιC0 I_C := by
505	  rw [horseshoeβ, biprod.lift_snd]
506	
507	/-- The augmentation composes to zero with the first horseshoe differential, so it descends to a
508	chain map from `B` (in degree 0) into the middle complex. -/
509	lemma horseshoeβ_comp_d :
510	    horseshoeβ hses I_A I_C ≫ twistedBiprodD (horseshoeτ hses I_A I_C) 0 = 0 := by
511	  have e : ses.g ≫ ιC0 I_C ≫ horseshoeτZero hses I_A I_C
512	      = -(horseshoeβ₁ hses I_A ≫ I_A.cocomplex.d 0 (0 + 1)) := by
513	    rw [ιC0_comp_τZero, Preadditive.comp_neg, g_comp_horseshoeH]
514	  have e2 : ses.g ≫ ιC0 I_C ≫ I_C.cocomplex.d 0 (0 + 1) = 0 := by
515	    have h0 : ιC0 I_C ≫ I_C.cocomplex.d 0 (0 + 1) = 0 := I_C.ι_f_zero_comp_complex_d
516	    rw [h0, comp_zero]
517	  apply biprod.hom_ext
518	  · simp only [Category.assoc, twistedBiprodD_fst, Preadditive.comp_add,
519	      horseshoeβ_fst_assoc, horseshoeβ_snd_assoc, horseshoeτ_zero, zero_comp]
520	    rw [e, add_neg_cancel]
521	  · simp only [Category.assoc, twistedBiprodD_snd, horseshoeβ_snd_assoc, zero_comp]
522	    exact e2
523	
524	/-- Maps out of a `single₀` cochain complex are determined by their degree-`0` component. -/
525	private lemma single₀_hom_ext {X : 𝒜} {D : CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ}
526	    {g h : (CochainComplex.single₀ 𝒜).obj X ⟶ D} (h0 : g.f 0 = h.f 0) : g = h := by
527	  apply (CochainComplex.fromSingle₀Equiv D X).injective
528	  ext
529	  exact h0
530	
531	/-- The horseshoe augmentation packaged as a chain map `(single₀ B) ⟶ I_B`, with degree-`0`
532	component the augmentation `β : B ⟶ I_A^0 ⊞ I_C^0`. -/
533	noncomputable def horseshoeι :
534	    (CochainComplex.single₀ 𝒜).obj ses.X₂ ⟶ horseshoeMid hses I_A I_C :=
535	  (CochainComplex.fromSingle₀Equiv (horseshoeMid hses I_A I_C) ses.X₂).symm
536	    ⟨horseshoeβ hses I_A I_C, by
537	      show horseshoeβ hses I_A I_C ≫
538	          (twistedBiprod (horseshoeτ hses I_A I_C) (horseshoeτ_cocycle hses I_A I_C)).d 0 1 = 0
539	      rw [twistedBiprod_d]
540	      exact horseshoeβ_comp_d hses I_A I_C⟩
541	
542	@[simp]
543	lemma horseshoeι_f_zero : (horseshoeι hses I_A I_C).f 0 = horseshoeβ hses I_A I_C := by
544	  simp [horseshoeι, CochainComplex.fromSingle₀Equiv]
545	
546	/-- The augmentation `β : B ⟶ I_A^0 ⊞ I_C^0` is a monomorphism (the base stage of the horseshoe
547	recursion: `mono_biprod_lift_factorThru_of_exact` applied to the original short exact sequence). -/
548	lemma mono_horseshoeβ : Mono (horseshoeβ hses I_A I_C) := by
549	  haveI := hses.mono_f
550	  haveI : Injective (I_A.cocomplex.X 0) := I_A.injective 0
551	  -- The domain of `I_A.ι.f 0` is `((single₀).obj ses.X₁).X 0`, not syntactically `ses.X₁`; we
552	  -- ascribe the clean domain so the `Mono` instance matches the lemma's `α : S.X₁ ⟶ P`.
553	  haveI : Mono (show ses.X₁ ⟶ I_A.cocomplex.X 0 from I_A.ι.f 0) :=
554	    mono_of_isLimit_fork I_A.isLimitKernelFork
555	  haveI : Mono (show ses.X₃ ⟶ I_C.cocomplex.X 0 from I_C.ι.f 0) :=
556	    mono_of_isLimit_fork I_C.isLimitKernelFork
557	  exact mono_biprod_lift_factorThru_of_exact hses.exact
558	    (show ses.X₁ ⟶ I_A.cocomplex.X 0 from I_A.ι.f 0)
559	    (show ses.X₃ ⟶ I_C.cocomplex.X 0 from I_C.ι.f 0)
560	
561	/-- The horseshoe left square: `I_A.ι ≫ (I_A → I_B) = (single₀ A → single₀ B) ≫ horseshoeι`. -/
562	lemma horseshoeφ_comm₁₂ :
563	    I_A.ι ≫ (horseshoeSES hses I_A I_C).f =
564	      (ses.map (CochainComplex.single₀ 𝒜)).f ≫ horseshoeι hses I_A I_C := by
565	  apply single₀_hom_ext
566	  change (I_A.ι ≫ twistedBiprodInl (horseshoeτ hses I_A I_C)
567	      (horseshoeτ_cocycle hses I_A I_C)).f 0 =
568	    ((CochainComplex.single₀ 𝒜).map ses.f ≫ horseshoeι hses I_A I_C).f 0
569	  rw [HomologicalComplex.comp_f, HomologicalComplex.comp_f, twistedBiprodInl_f,
570	    CochainComplex.single₀_map_f_zero, horseshoeι_f_zero, horseshoeβ]
571	  -- The biproduct projections from `twistedBiprod` carry a `(twistedBiprod).X 0`-flavoured
572	  -- domain; ascribe the clean biproduct so the `biprod.*` simp lemmas fire.
573	  change I_A.ι.f 0 ≫ (biprod.inl : I_A.cocomplex.X 0 ⟶ I_A.cocomplex.X 0 ⊞ I_C.cocomplex.X 0) =
574	    ses.f ≫ biprod.lift (horseshoeβ₁ hses I_A) (ses.g ≫ ιC0 I_C)
575	  apply biprod.hom_ext <;> simp [f_comp_horseshoeβ₁, reassoc_of% ses.zero]
576	
577	/-- The horseshoe right square: `horseshoeι ≫ (I_B → I_C) = (single₀ B → single₀ C) ≫ I_C.ι`. -/
578	lemma horseshoeφ_comm₂₃ :
579	    horseshoeι hses I_A I_C ≫ (horseshoeSES hses I_A I_C).g =
580	      (ses.map (CochainComplex.single₀ 𝒜)).g ≫ I_C.ι := by
581	  apply single₀_hom_ext
582	  change (horseshoeι hses I_A I_C ≫
583	      twistedBiprodSnd (horseshoeτ hses I_A I_C) (horseshoeτ_cocycle hses I_A I_C)).f 0 =
584	    ((CochainComplex.single₀ 𝒜).map ses.g ≫ I_C.ι).f 0
585	  rw [HomologicalComplex.comp_f, HomologicalComplex.comp_f, twistedBiprodSnd_f,
586	    CochainComplex.single₀_map_f_zero, horseshoeι_f_zero, horseshoeβ]
587	  change biprod.lift (horseshoeβ₁ hses I_A) (ses.g ≫ ιC0 I_C) ≫
588	    (biprod.snd : I_A.cocomplex.X 0 ⊞ I_C.cocomplex.X 0 ⟶ I_C.cocomplex.X 0) = ses.g ≫ I_C.ι.f 0
589	  rw [biprod.lift_snd]
590	  rfl
591	
592	/-- The morphism of short complexes of cochain complexes
593	`(single₀ A → single₀ B → single₀ C) ⟶ (I_A → I_B → I_C)` whose outer verticals are the
594	resolution augmentations `I_A.ι`, `I_C.ι` and whose middle vertical is the horseshoe augmentation
595	`horseshoeι`. -/
596	noncomputable def horseshoeφ :
597	    ses.map (CochainComplex.single₀ 𝒜) ⟶ horseshoeSES hses I_A I_C :=
598	  ShortComplex.homMk I_A.ι (horseshoeι hses I_A I_C) I_C.ι
599	    (horseshoeφ_comm₁₂ hses I_A I_C) (horseshoeφ_comm₂₃ hses I_A I_C)
600	
601	@[simp] lemma horseshoeφ_τ₁ : (horseshoeφ hses I_A I_C).τ₁ = I_A.ι := rfl
602	@[simp] lemma horseshoeφ_τ₂ : (horseshoeφ hses I_A I_C).τ₂ = horseshoeι hses I_A I_C := rfl
603	@[simp] lemma horseshoeφ_τ₃ : (horseshoeφ hses I_A I_C).τ₃ = I_C.ι := rfl
604	
605	/-- The horseshoe augmentation `(single₀ B) ⟶ I_B` is a quasi-isomorphism: by the middle-term
606	quasi-isomorphism transfer `quasiIso_τ₂`, since the outer augmentations `I_A.ι`, `I_C.ι` are
607	quasi-isomorphisms (they are injective resolutions) and `β` is mono. -/
608	lemma quasiIso_horseshoeι : QuasiIso (horseshoeι hses I_A I_C) := by
609	  have key := HomologicalComplex.HomologySequence.quasiIso_τ₂ (horseshoeφ hses I_A I_C)

6	import Mathlib
7	import AlgebraicJacobian.Cohomology.HigherDirectImage
8	
9	/-!
10	# Čech computation of the higher direct images `Rⁱ f_*` (unconditional)
11	
12	This file constructs the higher derived direct images `Rⁱ f_* F` for `i ≥ 1`
13	**without appealing to injective resolutions** in the category of sheaves of
14	modules. The companion `Cohomology/HigherDirectImage.lean` defines `Rⁱ f_*` as a
15	right derived functor, which requires the ambient category of `O_X`-modules to
16	have enough injectives — a property not currently available for sheaves of
17	modules over a sheaf of rings whose value category varies over the site. The
18	Čech approach developed here sidesteps the issue: it computes `Rⁱ f_* F` as the
19	cohomology of an explicit complex built from the pushforwards of `F` over the
20	finite intersections of an affine open cover, producing an **unconditional**
21	construction of `Rⁱ f_*` for quasi-coherent `F` and separated quasi-compact `f`.
22	
23	Throughout, `f : X ⟶ S` is a quasi-compact, separated morphism of schemes (so
24	all finite intersections of an affine open cover of `X` are again affine), and
25	`F : X.Modules` is a quasi-coherent `O_X`-module. A base change of `f` along
26	`g : S' ⟶ S` is recorded by a cartesian square
27	```
28	  X' --g'--> X
29	  |f'        |f
30	  v          v
31	  S' --g---> S
32	```
33	with `F' = (g')^* F` the pullback of `F` to `X'`.
34	
35	The six main declarations are:
36	
37	* `AlgebraicGeometry.CechNerve` — the (augmented) Čech nerve of an affine open
38	  cover, an augmented cosimplicial object of `O_X`-modules.
39	* `AlgebraicGeometry.CechComplex` — the relative Čech complex in `QCoh(S)`, a
40	  cochain complex of `O_S`-modules whose degree-`p` term is the product of the
41	  pushforwards of `F` over the `(p+1)`-fold intersections of the cover.
42	* `AlgebraicGeometry.CechAcyclic.affine` — Čech acyclicity on affines: the Čech
43	  complex of a standard cover of an affine scheme has vanishing cohomology in
44	  all positive degrees (Serre vanishing for quasi-coherent sheaves on affines).
45	* `AlgebraicGeometry.cech_computes_higherDirectImage` — the cohomology of the
46	  relative Čech complex is canonically isomorphic to `Rⁱ f_* F` wherever the
47	  derived functor is defined.
48	
49	See `blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage.tex`.
50	
51	Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, §Čech cohomology of quasi-coherent
52	sheaves and §Quasi-coherence of higher direct images; Tags 02KE
53	(`lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent`), 02KG
54	(`lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero`).
55	-/
56	
57	universe u
58	
59	open CategoryTheory Limits
60	
61	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
62	
63	open Scheme.Modules
64	
65	variable {S S' X X' : Scheme.{u}}
66	
67	/- The Čech nerve `CechNerve` is *constructed* (no longer a hole) further below, once the
68	geometric backbone (`coverCechNerveOverAug`) and the push–pull functor (`pushPullFunctor`)
69	are in scope; see its definition just before `relativeCechComplexOfNerve`. -/
70	
71	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — scheme-level Čech nerve backbone
72	
73	The genuine construction of the {\v C}ech nerve `CechNerve` factors through two
74	ingredients that are independent of one another:
75	
76	* a *geometric* backbone — the augmented {\v C}ech nerve of the cover, an
77	  augmented simplicial **scheme** over `X` (the iterated fibre powers of
78	  `∐ᵢ Uᵢ` over `X`), which exists unconditionally because `Scheme` has all
79	  finite limits; and
80	* a *push-pull* functor `(Over X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ X.Modules`, `(Y, p) ↦ p_* p^* F`, that
81	  turns the simplicial scheme over `X` into the cosimplicial `O_X`-module
82	  `CechNerve`.
83	
84	The backbone (`coverArrow`, `coverCechNerve`) is built here axiom-clean. The
85	push-pull functor is the remaining gap: its `map_comp` requires the
86	`pushforwardComp` / `pullbackComp` coherence isomorphisms (the same coherence
87	quagmire active in `Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean`), so `CechNerve` itself is
88	left as the single genuine hole.
89	
90	Independently of the nerve, the passage *from* an augmented cosimplicial
91	`O_X`-module *to* the relative {\v C}ech cochain complex in `QCoh(S)` is pure,
92	coherence-free plumbing (`relativeCechComplexOfNerve`): forget the augmentation,
93	push forward along `f` via `CosimplicialObject.whiskering`, and take the
94	alternating coface-map cochain complex. We record it here so that `CechComplex`
95	is *defined* in terms of `CechNerve` — closing `CechNerve` axiom-clean
96	immediately yields an axiom-clean `CechComplex`. -/
97	
98	/-- The arrow `∐ᵢ Uᵢ ⟶ X` (`Sigma.desc 𝒰.f`) attached to an open cover `𝒰` of a
99	scheme `X`. Its augmented {\v C}ech nerve is the geometric backbone of the
100	relative {\v C}ech complex. Project-local: packages the cover as a single arrow
101	so the existing `Arrow.augmentedCechNerve` machinery applies. -/
102	noncomputable def coverArrow (𝒰 : X.OpenCover) : Arrow Scheme.{u} :=
103	  Arrow.mk (Sigma.desc 𝒰.f)
104	
105	/-- The augmented {\v C}ech nerve of an open cover `𝒰`, as an augmented
106	simplicial scheme over `X`: in simplicial degree `p` it is the `(p+1)`-fold
107	fibre power of `∐ᵢ Uᵢ` over `X`, i.e. `∐_{(i₀,…,i_p)} U_{i₀} ×ₓ ⋯ ×ₓ U_{i_p}`,
108	with augmentation the cover map to `X`. Exists unconditionally because `Scheme`
109	has all finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks used by
110	`Arrow.augmentedCechNerve`). Project-local geometric backbone for `CechNerve`. -/
111	noncomputable def coverCechNerve (𝒰 : X.OpenCover) :
112	    SimplicialObject.Augmented Scheme.{u} :=
113	  (coverArrow 𝒰).augmentedCechNerve
114	
115	/-! ### Push–pull functor `G : (Over X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ X.Modules` — object and morphism bricks
116	
117	The geometric backbone above is lifted to a cosimplicial `O_X`-module by the
118	*relative-direct-image functor on the over-category*
119	```
120	  G : (Over X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ X.Modules,   (Y, p) ↦ p_* p^* F,
121	```
122	sending an `X`-scheme `p : Y ⟶ X` to the pushforward along `p` of the pullback
123	`p^* F`. We record here the two *pre-coherence* bricks of `G` — its action on
124	objects (`pushPullObj`, the planner's `Gobj`) and on morphisms (`pushPullMap`,
125	the planner's `Gmap`) — both axiom-clean and free of any functor law. The functor
126	laws `G(𝟙) = 𝟙` and `G(g ≫ h) = G(h) ≫ G(g)` are a *consumer* of the
127	pushforward/pullback composition coherence (`pushforwardComp` / `pullbackComp` and
128	their unitor/pentagon identities) and are deferred; see the note after
129	`pushPullMap`. -/
130	
131	/-- The object map of the push–pull functor `G : (Over X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ X.Modules`,
132	`(Y, p) ↦ p_* p^* F`. Sends an `X`-scheme `Y` (with structure map `Y.hom : Y.left
133	⟶ X`) to the pushforward along `Y.hom` of the pullback of `F`. Project-local
134	object brick of the {\v C}ech push–pull functor (the planner's `Gobj`). -/
135	noncomputable def pushPullObj (F : X.Modules) (Y : Over X) : X.Modules :=
136	  (pushforward Y.hom).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F)
137	
138	/-- The morphism map of the push–pull functor `G : (Over X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ X.Modules`. For a
139	morphism `g : Y₂ ⟶ Y₁` of `X`-schemes (so `g.left ≫ Y₁.hom = Y₂.hom`, the
140	over-triangle `Over.w g`), the contravariant functor produces the restriction
141	comparison `pushPullObj F Y₁ ⟶ pushPullObj F Y₂`. It is the three-step composite:
142	the unit `η` of the adjunction `pullback g.left ⊣ pushforward g.left`, the
143	pushforward comparison `(pushforwardComp g.left Y₁.hom).hom`, and the pushforward
144	of the pullback comparison `(pullbackComp g.left Y₁.hom).hom`, glued by two
145	`eqToHom` transports along the over-triangle `Over.w g`. No functor law is used:
146	this is a reusable pre-coherence brick (the planner's `Gmap`). -/
147	noncomputable def pushPullMap (F : X.Modules) {Y₁ Y₂ : Over X} (g : Y₂ ⟶ Y₁) :
148	    pushPullObj F Y₁ ⟶ pushPullObj F Y₂ :=
149	  (pushforward Y₁.hom).map
150	      ((pullbackPushforwardAdjunction g.left).unit.app
151	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y₁.hom).obj F)) ≫
152	    (pushforwardComp g.left Y₁.hom).hom.app
153	      ((Scheme.Modules.pullback g.left).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y₁.hom).obj F)) ≫
154	    eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (pushforward q).obj
155	      ((Scheme.Modules.pullback g.left).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y₁.hom).obj F)))
156	      (Over.w g)) ≫
157	    (pushforward Y₂.hom).map ((pullbackComp g.left Y₁.hom).hom.app F) ≫
158	    eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (pushforward Y₂.hom).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F))
159	      (Over.w g))
160	
161	/- **The functor laws `pushPullMap_id` / `pushPullMap_comp`.**
162	Assembling `pushPullObj` / `pushPullMap` into the functor `G : (Over X)ᵒᵖ ⥤
163	X.Modules` requires
164	```
165	  pushPullMap_id   : pushPullMap F (𝟙 Y) = 𝟙 (pushPullObj F Y)          -- DONE (below)
166	  pushPullMap_comp : pushPullMap F (g ≫ h) = pushPullMap F h ≫ pushPullMap F g  -- remaining
167	```
168	Both are pure pushforward/pullback *coherence* statements with no sectionwise
169	content of their own. The route is the adjunction-mate calculus: the
170	`unit ≫ pushforwardComp.hom` head of `pushPullMap` is governed by `unit_conjugateEquiv`
171	and `conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv` / `conjugateEquiv_pullbackId_hom`, and the `eqToHom`
172	transports are discharged against the pseudofunctor unitor/pentagon identities
173	(`pseudofunctor_right_unitality` for `id`, `pseudofunctor_associativity` for `comp`, all in
174	`Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/Modules/Sheaf.lean`).
175	
176	`pushPullMap_id` is closed axiom-clean below. A plain `simp` (even with the coherence lemmas)
177	makes no progress on the head `unit ≫ pushforwardComp.hom`, and the sectionwise `ext` route
178	exposes the pullback adjunction unit (not sectionwise trivial); the working proof instead routes
179	through `unit_conjugateEquiv` of the identity adjunction (`star`), the right-unitality of the
180	pullback comparison (`hru`), and a `Scheme.Modules.hom_ext; intro U; rfl` collapse of the
181	sectionwise-trivial pushforward coercion (`hpf`). `pushPullMap_comp` follows the same template
182	with `pseudofunctor_associativity` (the pentagon) in place of right-unitality — see the comment
183	above its (currently unfilled) statement. -/
184	
185	/-! ### Functor laws of the push–pull functor `G` -/
186	
187	/-- Identity law of the push–pull functor `G`. -/
188	lemma pushPullMap_id (F : X.Modules) (Y : Over X) :
189	    pushPullMap F (𝟙 Y) = 𝟙 (pushPullObj F Y) := by
190	  -- `star`: the unit-triangle for the identity adjunction `pullback 𝟙 ⊣ pushforward 𝟙`.
191	  have star := unit_conjugateEquiv (Adjunction.id (C := Y.left.Modules))
192	    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (𝟙 Y.left))
193	    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackId Y.left).hom ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F)
194	  rw [Scheme.Modules.conjugateEquiv_pullbackId_hom] at star
195	  simp only [Adjunction.id_unit, NatTrans.id_app, Functor.id_obj] at star
196	  -- `hru`: right-unitality of the pullback pseudofunctor, applied at `F`.
197	  have hru := Scheme.Modules.pseudofunctor_right_unitality (X := Y.left) (f := Y.hom)
198	  have hru2 := congrArg (fun t => NatTrans.app t F) hru
199	  simp only [NatTrans.comp_app, Functor.whiskerLeft_app, Functor.rightUnitor_hom_app] at hru2
200	  have hpf : (Scheme.Modules.pushforwardComp (𝟙 Y.left) Y.hom).hom.app
201	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (𝟙 Y.left)).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F)) ≫
202	      eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pushforward q).obj
203	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (𝟙 Y.left)).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F)))
204	        (Category.id_comp Y.hom)) =
205	      (Scheme.Modules.pushforward Y.hom).map ((Scheme.Modules.pushforwardId Y.left).hom.app
206	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (𝟙 Y.left)).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F))) := by
207	    apply Scheme.Modules.hom_ext
208	    intro U; rfl
209	  -- the unit zig-zag for the identity adjunction collapses on `M`
210	  have hzig : (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (𝟙 Y.left)).unit.app
211	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F) ≫
212	      (Scheme.Modules.pushforwardId Y.left).hom.app
213	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (𝟙 Y.left)).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F)) ≫
214	      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackId Y.left).hom.app ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F) =
215	      𝟙 ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F) := by
216	    have hnat := (Scheme.Modules.pushforwardId Y.left).hom.naturality
217	      ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackId Y.left).hom.app ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F))
218	    simp only [Functor.id_map] at hnat
219	    erw [← hnat, ← reassoc_of% star]
220	    exact Iso.inv_hom_id_app _ _
221	  -- the pullback comparison + the over-triangle transport collapse via right-unitality
222	  have hib_inner : (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (𝟙 Y.left) Y.hom).hom.app F ≫
223	      eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F) (Category.id_comp Y.hom)) =
224	      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackId Y.left).hom.app ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F) := by
225	    rw [eqToHom_app] at hru2
226	    rw [← hru2, ← Category.assoc, Iso.hom_inv_id_app]; simp
227	  have hib : (Scheme.Modules.pushforward Y.hom).map
228	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (𝟙 Y.left) Y.hom).hom.app F) ≫
229	      eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pushforward Y.hom).obj
230	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F)) (Category.id_comp Y.hom)) =
231	      (Scheme.Modules.pushforward Y.hom).map
232	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackId Y.left).hom.app ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F)) := by
233	    have he : eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pushforward Y.hom).obj
234	          ((Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F)) (Category.id_comp Y.hom)) =
235	        (Scheme.Modules.pushforward Y.hom).map
236	          (eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F)
237	            (Category.id_comp Y.hom))) := by
238	      rw [eqToHom_map]
239	    rw [he, ← Functor.map_comp]; exact congrArg _ hib_inner
240	  -- assemble
241	  simp only [pushPullMap, Over.id_left]
242	  erw [reassoc_of% hpf, hib, ← Functor.map_comp]
243	  erw [hzig, CategoryTheory.Functor.map_id]; rfl
244	
245	/- **Composition law of the push–pull functor `G` (contravariant) — remaining.**
246	The identity law `pushPullMap_id` above is closed axiom-clean by the mate calculus
247	(`unit_conjugateEquiv` + `conjugateEquiv_pullbackId_hom` + `pseudofunctor_right_unitality`).
248	The composition law
249	```
250	  pushPullMap_comp : pushPullMap F (g ≫ h) = pushPullMap F h ≫ pushPullMap F g
251	```
252	follows the *same* template one step up — but with the pentagon
253	`Scheme.Modules.pseudofunctor_associativity (f := g.left) (g := h.left) (h := Y₁.hom)`
254	(verified to typecheck) in place of `pseudofunctor_right_unitality`, the composite-adjunction
255	unit expansion `Adjunction.comp_unit_app` for `η_{g.left ≫ h.left}`, and
256	`Adjunction.unit_naturality` to slide the inner unit past the outer pushforward. The pushforward
257	coercions (`pushforwardComp ≫ eqToHom`) collapse sectionwise by `Scheme.Modules.hom_ext; intro U; rfl`
258	exactly as the `hpf` helper of `pushPullMap_id`. It is a longer (~150-LOC) pentagon calculation and is
259	left for a focused follow-up pass; assembling `pushPullFunctor` needs it together with `pushPullMap_id`.
260	
261	The mate core that splits the composite unit is isolated as the reusable lemma
262	`pushPull_unit_mate` below (axiom-clean): it rewrites the head
263	`p_*(η^f) ≫ pushforwardComp` as `η^{f≫p} ≫ (f≫p)_*(pullbackComp⁻¹)`.
264	
265	**iter-271 breakthrough — the kernel `whnf` wall is now BYPASSED.** The earlier
266	blocker was a kernel `whnf` blow-up when cancelling `pushPullMap`'s two over-triangle
267	`eqToHom` transports *in situ* (at the concrete `pushforward`/`pullback` comparison
268	objects). The reusable brick `pushPull_transport_cancel` below resolves it: it states
269	the cancellation with the over-triangle equality `h : gl ≫ p₁ = p₂` as a **free
270	hypothesis**, proven by a single `subst h` on *abstract* objects (transports become
271	`eqToHom rfl = 𝟙`, kernel-cheap), and is applied to `pushPullMap` via `erw` — which
272	rewrites the tail without forcing the kernel to unfold the comparison objects. (Plain
273	`rw` does not fire: `SheafOfModules` comps are defeq-not-syntactic, so `erw` is
274	mandatory.) Applying `erw [pushPull_transport_cancel …]` to all three `pushPullMap`
275	occurrences (LHS once, RHS twice) was verified to fire with NO kernel blow-up, leaving
276	both sides transport-light. `pushforwardComp` is `Iso.refl` (strictly functorial), so
277	its comparison factor is `𝟙` and is absorbed by the same `erw`.
278	
279	**Remaining work for `pushPullMap_comp` — a clean post-`subst` pentagon (no kernel
280	issues).** After the three `erw`s, `subst`-ing the two free over-triangles (via a
281	generalised auxiliary lemma with `p₂, p₃` and the over-triangles as free hypotheses,
282	mirroring `pushPull_transport_cancel`) clears the remaining transports except the
283	single associativity cell `eqToHom ((kl≫gl₂)≫p₁ = kl≫(gl₂≫p₁))`. The residual goal is
284	the pure pseudofunctor pentagon: it closes by `pushPull_unit_mate kl gl₂` (to convert
285	the composite unit `η^{kl≫gl₂}`) together with
286	`Scheme.Modules.pseudofunctor_associativity (f := kl) (g := gl₂) (h := p₁)` — whose four
287	`pullbackComp` 2-cells (`pullbackComp kl (gl₂≫p₁)`, `pullbackComp gl₂ p₁`,
288	`pullbackComp kl gl₂`, `pullbackComp (kl≫gl₂) p₁`) match exactly the four appearing in
289	the goal. The decompositions `pushforward (a≫b) = pushforward a ⋙ pushforward b` and
290	`(pushforward (a≫b)).map φ = (pushforward b).map ((pushforward a).map φ)` both hold by
291	`rfl`. The only friction left is the whiskered-pentagon + `eqToHom` bookkeeping (the
292	standard defeq-not-syntactic `erw` grind), a ~60-100 LOC follow-up; the kernel obstacle
293	that blocked five prior iterations is gone. -/
294	
295	/-- **Base-change unit (mate) identity for the push–pull head.**
296	For composable scheme morphisms `f : A ⟶ B`, `p : B ⟶ Z` and `N : Z.Modules`, the
297	adjunction unit at `N` for `p` followed by the *head* of `pushPullMap` (the
298	pushforward of the unit for `f`, then the pushforward comparison) equals the unit
299	for the composite `f ≫ p` followed by the pushforward of the inverse pullback
300	comparison. This is the mate-calculus core that converts the single-morphism unit
301	`η^{f≫p}` into the iterated units `η^p`, `η^f`; it is the reusable ingredient that
302	the functoriality (pentagon) law of `pushPullMap` repeatedly consumes when
303	splitting a composite unit. Project-local supplement. -/
304	lemma pushPull_unit_mate {A B Z : Scheme.{u}} (f : A ⟶ B) (p : B ⟶ Z)
305	    (N : Z.Modules) :
306	    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction p).unit.app N ≫
307	        (Scheme.Modules.pushforward p).map
308	          ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f).unit.app
309	            ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p).obj N)) ≫
310	        (Scheme.Modules.pushforwardComp f p).hom.app
311	          ((Scheme.Modules.pullback f).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p).obj N)) =
312	      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (f ≫ p)).unit.app N ≫
313	        (Scheme.Modules.pushforward (f ≫ p)).map
314	          ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp f p).inv.app N) := by
315	  have key := unit_conjugateEquiv
316	    ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction p).comp
317	      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f))
318	    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (f ≫ p))
319	    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp f p).inv N
320	  rw [Scheme.Modules.conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv, Adjunction.comp_unit_app] at key
321	  simpa only [Category.assoc] using key
322	
323	/-- **Over-triangle transport cancellation for the push–pull tail** (kernel-cheap
324	generalised form). The morphism map `pushPullMap` glues its pullback-comparison leg
325	to the target object `pushPullObj F Y₂` by two `eqToHom` coercions along the
326	over-triangle `g.left ≫ Y₁.hom = Y₂.hom`. Cancelling those coercions *in situ*
327	(at the concrete pushforward/pullback objects) provokes a kernel `whnf` blow-up.
328	This lemma states the cancellation **with the over-triangle equality as a free
329	hypothesis** `h : gl ≫ p₁ = p₂`, so the proof is a single `subst h` (after which
330	the transports become `eqToHom rfl = 𝟙` and vanish — kernel-cheap) followed by
331	`simp`. Applying it to `pushPullMap` via `rw` rewrites the tail without forcing the
332	kernel to unfold the comparison objects: the over-triangle leg
333	`eqToHom ≫ (pushforward p₂).map (pullbackComp).hom ≫ eqToHom` collapses to the
334	transport-light `(pushforward (gl ≫ p₁)).map (pullbackComp).hom ≫ eqToHom`, the
335	single residual `eqToHom` carrying the unavoidable object identification of the
336	codomain `pushPullObj F Y₂`. Reusable pre-coherence brick for `pushPullMap_comp`. -/
337	lemma pushPull_transport_cancel {Y₁ Y₂ : Scheme.{u}}
338	    (gl : Y₂ ⟶ Y₁) (p₁ : Y₁ ⟶ X) (p₂ : Y₂ ⟶ X)
339	    (h : gl ≫ p₁ = p₂) (F : X.Modules) :
340	    eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pushforward q).obj
341	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback gl).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p₁).obj F))) h) ≫
342	      (Scheme.Modules.pushforward p₂).map ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp gl p₁).hom.app F) ≫

8	/-!
9	# Higher direct images `Rⁱ f_*` of quasi-coherent sheaves (`i ≥ 1`)
10	
11	This file treats the higher derived direct images `Rⁱ f_* F` for `i ≥ 1`,
12	complementing the `i = 0` (direct-image) case of `Cohomology/FlatBaseChange.lean`.
13	
14	Throughout, `f : X ⟶ S` is a morphism of schemes and `F : X.Modules` a sheaf of
15	modules on `X`. The `i`-th higher direct image `Rⁱ f_* F` is the `i`-th right
16	derived functor of the pushforward functor `f_*` applied to `F`.
17	
18	This file supplies the single declaration needed downstream by the Čech
19	computation of higher direct images:
20	
21	* `AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage` — the higher direct image `Rⁱ f_* F`,
22	  the derived-functor object that `cech_computes_higherDirectImage` compares the
23	  Čech complex against.
24	
25	See `blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_HigherDirectImage.tex`.
26	
27	Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Definition of `Rⁱ f_*`.
28	-/
29	
30	universe u
31	
32	open CategoryTheory Limits
33	
34	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
35	
36	open Scheme.Modules
37	
38	variable {S X : Scheme.{u}}
39	
40	/-- The `i`-th higher direct image `Rⁱ f_* F` of a sheaf of modules `F` on `X`
41	along a morphism `f : X ⟶ S`, defined as the `i`-th right derived functor of the
42	pushforward functor `f_*` applied to `F`.
43	
44	For `i = 0` this recovers the ordinary pushforward `R⁰ f_* F = f_* F`.
45	
46	Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Definition of `Rⁱ f_*`. -/
47	noncomputable def higherDirectImage [HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]
48	    (f : X ⟶ S) (i : ℕ) (F : X.Modules) : S.Modules :=
49	  ((pushforward f).rightDerived i).obj F
50	
51	end AlgebraicGeometry
